questions.question: State and explain the Hull-Dobell Theorem for achieving maximum period in Linear Congruential Generators.
keyPoints: State the theorem conditions: gcd(c,m)=1, a-1 divisible by all prime factors of m, a-1 divisible by 4 if m divisible by 4
Explain that these are necessary and sufficient conditions
Mention that full period = m when all conditions satisfied
Note difference for multiplicative LCG (period m-1)

questions.question: Explain why Linear Congruential Generators are NOT suitable for cryptographic applications despite their efficiency.
keyPoints: LCG uses linear recurrence - inherently predictable
Given few consecutive outputs, parameters can be recovered
Mention lattice reduction/Berlekamp-Welch attacks
Explain that even large parameters don't help against these attacks
Compare with CSPRNG requirements

questions.question: What is the spectral test for evaluating LCG quality? Explain its significance.
keyPoints: Spectral test checks uniformity in k-dimensional space
Poor LCGs produce points on limited hyperplanes (spectral lines)
More hyperplanes = better uniformity = higher k-dimensional quality
Explain spectral defect - only m^(1/k) distinct k-tuples possible
Mention it complements other statistical tests

questions.question: Derive the mathematical formula for Linear Congruential Generator and explain each parameter's role.
keyPoints: Write formula: Xₙ₊₁ = (aXₙ + c) mod m
Explain: a = multiplier determining randomness quality
Explain: c = increment (addend)
Explain: m = modulus (bounds period)
Explain: X₀ = seed (initial state)

questions.question: Differentiate between additive and multiplicative LCG. Which achieves larger period and why?
keyPoints: Additive LCG: c ≠ 0, formula: Xₙ₊₁ = (aXₙ + c) mod m
Multiplicative LCG: c = 0, formula: Xₙ₊₁ = (aXₙ) mod m
Additive can achieve period m (full state space)
Multiplicative limited to m-1 (0 is absorbing state)
Explain why prime m and primitive root a matter for multiplicative

questions.question: Explain the 'saddle point' or hyperplane defect in LCGs with an example.
keyPoints: LCG outputs lie on a finite number of parallel hyperplanes
This creates visible patterns in output
In k dimensions, only m^(1/k) distinct k-tuples possible
Example: in 2D, points form line patterns
Spectral test quantifies this defect

questions.question: The LCG parameters are m=31, a=3, c=0, X₀=1. Generate the first 5 pseudo-random numbers and determine the period.
keyPoints: Show iterative calculation: X₁=3×1 mod 31=3
Period is 30 (m-1) since multiplicative with prime m and primitive root a

questions.question: A cryptographer proposes using LCG with large parameters (m ≈ 2²⁵⁶) as a PRNG for key generation. Analyze this proposal from security perspective and suggest appropriate countermeasures if any.
keyPoints: LCG remains insecure regardless of parameter size due to linear structure
Explain that lattice reduction attacks work in polynomial time
With 256-bit parameters, need only ~256 consecutive outputs
Attack complexity: O(k³) where k is parameter bit-length
Recommend CSPRNGs: Blum Blum Shub, Yarrow, /dev/urandom
Mention that LCG can be used as Stream cipher with proper design (not standard LCG)

questions.question: Design a secure key exchange protocol using LCG concepts with modifications to make it cryptographically secure. Explain your design decisions and security properties.
keyPoints: Standard LCG is insecure due to linearity
Propose modifications: use non-linear combination, incorporate feedback
Option 1: Use multiple LCGs with different parameters and combine outputs
Option 2: Apply cryptographic hash to LCG output
Option 3: Use LCG parameters as private key in larger mathematical problem
Discuss forward secrecy and resistance to attacks
Compare with DH key exchange approach

questions.question: Compare and contrast Linear Congruential Generators with Linear Feedback Shift Registers (LFSR) in terms of mathematical structure, period, and cryptographic applications.
keyPoints: LCG uses modular arithmetic in Z_m (integers)
LFSR uses arithmetic in GF(2) (binary/bits)
Both are linear recurrences but over different fields
LCG period bounded by m, LFSR period by 2^n-1 (max)
LFSR also cryptographically weak (can be broken by Berlekamp-Massey)
Neither suitable for cryptography without non-linear combining
Both useful in stream ciphers when properly combined
